mnemonics.title: Green Computing Goals
mnemonics.content: The acronym ERRS helps remember the four main goals: Energy efficiency, Reduced e-waste, Resource conservation, and Sustainability in manufacturing. Think: 'Eco-friendly, Reduce, Reuse, Save'.

mnemonics.title: Data Center Efficiency
mnemonics.content: Remember PUE = Total Facility Energy ÷ IT Equipment Energy. A good PUE is close to 1.0. Use the phrase 'Perfect Under 1.5' - aim for PUE below 1.5.

mnemonics.title: AI Carbon Footprint Factors
mnemonics.content: Remember the 'DATA' mnemonic: Dataset size, Algorithm complexity, Training iterations, and Architecture depth. Larger values in any factor increase carbon emissions.

acronyms.usage: Measures data center energy efficiency; lower is better, ideal is 1.0
Discarded electronic devices requiring special disposal
Technology that can have significant carbon footprint when training large models
Method to evaluate environmental impact from raw materials to disposal
Gases like CO2 that contribute to climate change from IT operations
Company offering cloud computing services with environmental considerations
Includes hidden environmental costs in IT decisions
Inverse of PUE; measures IT equipment efficiency as percentage

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pie chart where the IT equipment slice (computation) is tiny (5%) and the rest (cooling, power, storage) is huge (95%). This visual shows why PUE improvement is critical.
Picture one large house (physical server) divided into many small apartments (virtual servers), each with its own resources but sharing common infrastructure like electricity and water.
Think of a conveyor belt: raw materials → manufacturing → distribution → use → disposal. Each stage has environmental costs, but disposal into landfill creates the biggest problems.
Visualize a scale: on one side, simple calculations (like calculator) - very light; on the other, training GPT - extremely heavy, like a elephant. The scale dramatically tilts.

analogies.analogy: A data center is like a city that never sleeps - it needs constant power for computing (like homes), cooling (like air conditioning in summer), and lighting (like streetlights). Most energy goes to support systems, not actual work.

Virtualisation is like a carpool lane - instead of one person in each car (one server per task), you pack multiple people (virtual machines) into fewer vehicles (physical servers), reducing traffic (energy) and parking space (hardware).

Carbon offsetting is like paying someone else to recycle your plastic - you still created waste, but you're funding a solution to compensate for your impact. It's not a perfect solution but helps reduce net impact.

Jevons Paradox is like making cars more fuel-efficient - you'd think we'd use less gas, but because driving becomes cheaper, people drive more, potentially using more fuel overall. More efficient technology can lead to more technology use.
